coredata.dc:description: Red-emitting carbon dots (R-CDs) have garnered significant interest owing to its exceptional fluorescence (FL) characteristics. In this study, we present a one-pot solvothermal synthesis for producing biomass-derived R-CDs, utilizing Birch leaves as the primary raw material. The R-CDs display high solubility in ethanol and demonstrate deep-red emission at 625 nm with narrowband characteristics. After conducting a selectivity assessment with different metal ions, it is observed that R-CDs demonstrate exceptional sensitivity and specificity specifically to Fe3+. This demonstrates a linear relationship up to a concentration of 500 μM, with a detection limit of 0.302 μM (S/N=3). In this research, we created photoluminescent and transparent composites made from polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) with embedded fluorescent R-CDs, using the solution casting method for fabrication. The synthesized R-CDs exhibit robust and consistent FL in solution, whereas, the R-CDs@PVA nanocomposites (NCs) films are transparent and exceptionally flexible. The optimized 0.08 wt% R-CDs demonstrated the most effective ultra violet (UV) blockage across the UV-A (76.84 %), UV-B (74.86 %), and UV-C (78.46 %) region. However, PVA alone absorbed < 25 % of the light in all UV regions. The study explored the shape memory and FL characteristics of the R-CDs@PVA NCs changed under varying conditions such as water, temperature and pH. In alkaline conditions, the FL intensity of the R-CDs@PVA NCs increased due to the deprotonation of the R-CDs surface. In addition, R-CDs dispersed within PVA exhibit superior ink-free patterned substrates, proving to be advantageous for non-destructive template relief printing. Furthermore, R-CDs serve as effective anti-oxidants, combating 2,2-Diphenyl-1-picrylhydrazyl (DPPH) scavenging activity and mitigating the oxidative stress (OS) in red blood cells (RBCs) induced by sodium nitrite (NaNO2). They aid in the restoration of essential stress indicators, encompassing lipid peroxidation (LPO), protein carbonyl content (PCC), total thiol (TT), superoxide dismutase (SOD), and catalase (CAT). Furthermore, R-CDs restored the damaged liver, kidney, heart, and pancreas by regulating biochemical parameters. Furthermore, incorporated R-CDs into red light-emitting diodes (R-LEDs) and white light-emitting diodes (W-LEDs), thereby enhancing plant growth efficiency. The results of this study highlight the possible of R-CDs as non-toxic, UV blocking agents suitable for developing edible coatings and packaging. These properties suggest their applicability in the food industry, anti-counterfeiting (AC) measures, and biomedical applications.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Red-emitting carbon dots (R-CDs) have garnered significant interest owing to its exceptional fluorescence (FL) characteristics. In this study, we present a one-pot solvothermal synthesis for producing biomass-derived R-CDs, utilizing Birch leaves as the primary raw material. The R-CDs display high solubility in ethanol and demonstrate deep-red emission at 625 nm with narrowband characteristics. After conducting a selectivity assessment with different metal ions, it is observed that R-CDs demonstrate exceptional sensitivity and specificity specifically to Fe3+. This demonstrates a linear relationship up to a concentration of 500 μM, with a detection limit of 0.302 μM (S/N=3). In this research, we created photoluminescent and transparent composites made from polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) with embedded fluorescent R-CDs, using the solution casting method for fabrication. The synthesized R-CDs exhibit robust and consistent FL in solution, whereas, the R-CDs@PVA nanocomposites (NCs) films are transparent and exceptionally flexible. The optimized 0.08 wt% R-CDs demonstrated the most effective ultra violet (UV) blockage across the UV-A (76.84 %), UV-B (74.86 %), and UV-C (78.46 %) region. However, PVA alone absorbed < 25 % of the light in all UV regions. The study explored the shape memory and FL characteristics of the R-CDs@PVA NCs changed under varying conditions such as water, temperature and pH. In alkaline conditions, the FL intensity of the R-CDs@PVA NCs increased due to the deprotonation of the R-CDs surface. In addition, R-CDs dispersed within PVA exhibit superior ink-free patterned substrates, proving to be advantageous for non-destructive template relief printing. Furthermore, R-CDs serve as effective anti-oxidants, combating 2,2-Diphenyl-1-picrylhydrazyl (DPPH) scavenging activity and mitigating the oxidative stress (OS) in red blood cells (RBCs) induced by sodium nitrite (NaNO2). They aid in the restoration of essential stress indicators, encompassing lipid peroxidation (LPO), protein carbonyl content (PCC), total thiol (TT), superoxide dismutase (SOD), and catalase (CAT). Furthermore, R-CDs restored the damaged liver, kidney, heart, and pancreas by regulating biochemical parameters. Furthermore, incorporated R-CDs into red light-emitting diodes (R-LEDs) and white light-emitting diodes (W-LEDs), thereby enhancing plant growth efficiency. The results of this study highlight the possible of R-CDs as non-toxic, UV blocking agents suitable for developing edible coatings and packaging. These properties suggest their applicability in the food industry, anti-counterfeiting (AC) measures, and biomedical applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) have gained attention for their unique benefits, including easy availability, excellent biocompatibility, low cytotoxicity and high quantum yield (QY), making them valuable in bioimaging fields. While CDs with high QY in green and blue spectra are well-studied, achieving bright emissions in the deep red/near-infrared (NIR) range remains a challenge [1]. Longer wavelength emissions, like those in the deep red/NIR range or through 2-photon FL, consistently improve optical imaging quality. The deep red/NIR range, in particular, reduces light scattering by tissues and autofluorescence, significantly enhancing spatial resolution and imaging contrast [2]. The limited availability of studies on CDs emitting in the deep red/NIR spectrum is primarily owing to its low QY, reliance on hindering deep tissue imaging and visible light excitation. A concise summary of reported R-CDs from various precursors and their optical properties is presented in Table 1 [3–17].

Heavy metals pose a serious environmental threat to plants, animals, and humans due to their widespread use in industrial and domestic field. Regular monitoring of these potentially harmful ions is essential. Ferric ion (Fe³⁺) is particularly important, as it plays key roles in both environmental and biological systems. Iron is a key element in molecules like hemoglobin and myoglobin and is involved in various enzyme activities. It plays a crucial role in the physiological and chemical functions of living organisms, participating in essential processes such as cellular metabolism, nucleic acid synthesis, enzymatic catalysis, and electron transport. Fe³⁺ ions primarily accumulate in the spleen, liver, and bone marrow, where they form complexes with ferritin [18]. Imbalances in Fe³⁺, whether excess or deficiency, can cause various disorders. Excessive Fe³⁺ is associated with cancers and impaired functions of the pancreas, lungs, and heart. Conversely, a deficiency in Fe³⁺ ions can lead to anaemia. Thus, detecting Fe³⁺ in the environment and evaluating its health impacts are crucial. This underscores the need for a sensitive, selective, and safe detection method. FL spectroscopy, known for its high sensitivity, ease of use, and reproducibility, has become prominent. In this context, CDs offer a promising alternative to traditional FL probes for detecting heavy metal ions [19,20].

Shape memory polymers (SMPs) are well-known for their ability to undergo significant shape changes rapidly in response to stimuli namely heat, light, solvents, and electric/magnetic fields. Key to developing SMPs with effective shape recovery are stable cross-linked networks and incorporated switching segments. Recently, various SMP composites have been developed, each showing distinct responses to different stimuli. Polymer-based SMPs with a PVA matrix are increasingly favoured for their ease of processing, non-toxicity, biocompatibility, and promising mechanical properties [21]. PVA-based SMPs hold great potential for water-induced shape changes, as water is a simple and gentle stimulus, enabling versatile applications. These benefits make PVA-based SMPs well-suited to meet the needs of various applications, especially in biomedical engineering [22].

To improve shape recovery in PVA-based SMPs, studies have highlighted the effectiveness of enhancing hydrogen bonding within polymer chains by adding nanofillers. Various fillers, such as polyaniline fibers, aluminum oxide, carboxymethyl cellulose sodium and graphene oxide, have been used for this purpose [23,24]. Historically, nanofiller-induced hydrogen bond crosslinking in PVA-based SMPs has been sensitive to water, leading to decreased stiffness and mechanical stability but improved shape recovery. This property has driven research into water-induced shape memory in PVA and CD composites. However, the blue emission from these PVA@CD constructs restricts their use in biomedical applications. This limitation occurs because biological environments naturally exhibit blue autofluorescence when CDs-containing SMPs are excited, complicating the accurate monitoring of shape recovery. Therefore, developing water-activated SMPs that emit long-wavelength FL is crucial [25].

UV radiation is classified into three types based on wavelength: UV-A (320–400 nm), UV-B (280–320 nm) and UV-C (100–280 nm). Excessive UV exposure can significantly impact human health and physiological processes due to photochemical reactions. UV-A leads to melanin production, skin darkening, photoaging, and discoloration by penetrating the dermis. UV-B, with higher energy, can cause severe sunburns, erythema, and blistering. UV-C is mostly absorbed by the ozone layer and poses fewer health risks. However, UV-A and UV-B have the most pronounced negative effects on human health and the environment, particularly on polymer films used in various industries [26,27]. Prolonged UV exposure (200–400 nm) generates free radicals in polymers, accelerating chain damage and causing noticeable deformation and turned yellow. Despite improvements in UV resistance, further work is needed to achieve superior transparency and UV protection [28].

Counterfeiting or forgery involves unauthorized use of content/work or copying, threatening governments, consumers, companies, and impacting the economy of the global. This illegal practice has grown, affecting everything from everyday goods and pharmaceuticals to high-end items, currency and documents [29]. The rise in counterfeiting is largely due to technological advances, global trade, weak law enforcement, the Internet, and high consumer demand. Forgery of documents and banknotes can undermine the integrity and value of currency and certificates, eroding public trust. To address this, it is essential to improve current AC techniques and develop new, hard-to-replicate, yet easily identifiable methods [30,31].

Food protective coatings and films should be to be composed of biodegradable, biocompatible, and sustainable materials that provide antibacterial, UV-blocking, and antioxidant qualities. To guarantee minimal permeability to oxygen and moisture, they also need mechanically strong matrices with hydrophobic groups, including natural and synthetic polymers [32]. To improve food packaging, polymer NCs can enhance recyclability while providing essential features such as antifungal, antimicrobial, and oxygen barrier properties, as well as appearance and colour. This can be achieved by integrating nanoscale fillers with natural (e.g., chitosan, alginate, cellulose, starch) and synthetic polymers (e.g., polyethylene, PVA, polypropylene, polyvinyl chloride). Additionally, to address UV exposure, UV-absorbing compounds are added to materials like collagen, cellulose, polyimides, and PVA to prevent degradation and improve UV shielding. PVA is particularly notable for being edible, biodegradable, non-toxic, and environmentally friendly [33,34]. The FDA has approved PVA for human clinical trials. Its exceptional mechanical strength, film-forming ability, and oxygen barrier properties make it suitable for food product coatings and packaging. However, PVA faces challenges such as poor heat stability, susceptibility to biological degradation, high moisture absorption, and limited UV protection. To address these issues and enhance performance, PVA is combined with both inorganic and organic components. The hydrogen bonding groups in the PVA structure aid in incorporating functional NCs, thereby enhancing its overall properties [35].

Free radicals, produced through biological oxidation, play a role in cellular processes at low concentrations but can damage proteins, lipids, and DNA at higher levels, leading to cellular damage [36]. They are a key factor in the pathophysiology of conditions such as diabetes, angina, hypertension, myocardial infarction, cardiomyopathy, atherosclerosis, and thrombosis. Recent research also indicates that elevated ROS levels damage RBCs and induce eryptosis [37]. Other cells suffer from hypoxia and severe haemolytic anaemia due to eryptosis. Crucially, eryptosis-induced ROS in RBCs leads to hyperactivation of both primary and secondary haemostasis, resulting in the formation of abnormal clots in veins and arteries, known as thrombosis [38].

In this study, an eco-friendly one-step solvothermal synthesis process was used to prepare R-CDs. The unique red emission of these R-CDs significantly reduced background Fl interference and avoided the common issue of aggregation-induced FL quenching. These R-CDs show considerable potential for use in AC applications as fluorescent ink. Additionally, by incorporating the synthesized R-CDs into PVA films, we created fluorescent hybrid polymer films (R-CDs@PVA) with multifunctional properties, including flexibility, suitability for food packaging, UV-blocking capabilities, and shape memory characteristics.

Birch leaves were obtained from a local market in Tumkur, Karnataka, India, for the purpose of this study. Acetone, Ethanol, petroleum ether, ethyl acetate, PVA, Ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA), glucose. Filter membrane (0.45 µm) and dialysis membrane (cutoff molecular weight ∼3500). The analytical grade chemicals Cd(NO3)2.4 H2O (99.9 %), ZnCl2 (98.0 %) HgCl2 (99.0 %), (NH4)2Fe(SO4)2.6 H2O (99.90 %), NiCl2.6 H2O (99.90 %), anhydrous FeCl3 (97.0 %), CuSO4.6 H2O (99.99 %), Pb(NO3)2 (99.90 %), CoCl2.6 H2O (98.0 %), CrCl3 (99.9 %) were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich, India. HCl (0.1 M) and NaOH (0.1 M) solutions were used for pH adjustments. No additional purification was required for any of the compounds or reagents. only double-deionized (DDI) water is used during the experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of red emissive CDs (R-CDs) from Birch leaves
ce:section.ce:para: Key processes in the synthesis and preparation of the R-CDs are shown in Fig. 1 aI–VIII. The resulting morphological and chemical compositions are shown in Fig. 1 a I–IV. In brief, the sole starting material employed for synthesis is freshly harvested Birch leaves (as shown in Fig. 1 a I & V), which encompass a wide array of biomolecules. These comprise chlorophyll a, glycosides, flavonoids, fatty acids, essential oils, phenol carboxylic acids, vitamins and minerals. In Fig. 1 aI, the bottom right part shows the chlorophyll a molecular arrangement. Initially, the Birch leaves underwent heating at 100 °C for 4 h under room temperature (RT) to eliminate moisture ( Fig. 1 a II & VI). The dehydrated Birch leaves were subsequently pulverized into a powder and submerged in acetone (Fig. 1 a III & VII). The resulting acetone-soluble fraction was then transferred in an autoclave to undergo complete the final solvothermal carbonization reaction for 5 h at 120 °C (Fig. 1 a IV &VIII). Although the total output of the resultant R-CDs is only around 5 % of the initial raw material, which was dried Birch leaves, this moderate yield is not an important concern because the starting material is naturally abundant and has low environmental and economical. The organic residue that has been collected is also significant since it may be burned to recover heat without producing any further carbon dioxide emissions. The solubility of the synthesized R-CDs in different solvents is vital for practical applications, including printed electronics and usage in liquid environments. Therefore, we systematically investigated the solubility of R-CDs derived from Birch leaves and found that they can dissolve at a concentration of 16 g L−1 in acetone, over 4 g L−1 in methanol, dimethyl sulfoxide, toluene, and anisole, and an impressive 45 g L−1 in ethanol. As can be seen from the images displayed in Fig. 1 a, the R-CD in an ethanol solution at a solute concentration of 1.6 mg L−1 is somewhat brown in the daylight and turns bright red at 365 nm in the UV.

The Birch leaves were obtained from the western Himalayan region of India. Following this, they underwent a rinse with tap water, followed by drying at 90 °C for 3 h, and were subsequently pulverized into a powder. Subsequently, 10 mL of acetone is added with 0.5 g of the powdered dry Birch leaf. Following acetone extraction, the mixture is placed into a 50 mL poly(tetrafluoroethylene) Teflon© lined autoclave then heated for 4 h at 140 °C. The crude solution is centrifuged (10,000 rpm/10 min) to remove big particles once it has cooled to room temperature (RT). The resultant supernatant is then purified by column-based chromatography, using a mixture of petroleum ether as the solvent and ethyl acetate as the eluent, after being filtered through a 0.45 µm PTFE membrane. Throughout the chromatographic separation process, the volume ratio of petroleum ether to ethyl acetate in the eluent is initially established at 10:1 and subsequently modified to 10:5. The obtained R-CDs sample is dried by rotary evaporation at dynamic vacuum, then the resulting R-CDs powder afterwards re-suspended in ethanol for further uses. A schematic representation of the R-CDs fabrication procedure is shown in Fig. 1 a.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Formulation of anti-counterfeiting ink with R-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: For the formulation of the AC ink utilizing R-CDs, a blend comprising 5 g of PVA and R-CDs is prepared. Ensuring a uniform dispersion is essential, thus 0.5 g of R-CDs undergoes 60 min of sonication in the PVA matrix. After that, a vacuum pumping device is used to degas the solution for ten min in order to eliminate any air bubbles. The translucent solution obtained functions as security ink, ready for immediate application. Fig. 1 b illustrates a schematic illustration for designing the AC labels by utilizing screen-printing and brush mode technique using anti-counterfeit ink.

The fabrication process of R-CDs@PVA NCs entailed blending different concentrations (ranging from 0.02 to 0.08 wt%) of R-CDs into a PVA suspension. The procedure begins with the dispersion of 2 g of PVA in 20 mL of deionized (DI) water, which is then heated to 90 °C. Until a clear solution is obtained, vigorous stirring with a magnetic stirrer is continued for ∼30 min. The solution is then allowed to cool to RT (26 °C). The resulting PVA solution is then mixed with 1 mL of R-CDs solutions at the specified mass concentrations of 0, 0.02, 0.04, and 0.08 mg/mL. Until a homogenous solution is achieved, the mixture is continuously stirred and sonicated for 20 min. The resultant PVA or R-CDs@PVA solutions are subsequently transferred into a clear petri plate using a tiny pipette to yield 4 mL, which is then allowed to air dry at RT for 2 days. By carefully regulating the evaporation of water, this method helps films to form. The identical process is performed to prepare each R-CDs@PVA NC film. To maintain consistency, the thickness of the film is meticulously regulated by using standardized volumes of R-CDs@PVA solution and ensuring uniform film sizes throughout the preparation process. The thickness of the cast films is determined to be approximately 0.20 ± 0.02 mm using an LCD digital Vernier caliper. The samples are labeled as follows: PVA, R-CDs@PVA 0.02 wt%, R-CDs@PVA 0.04 wt%, R-CDs@PVA 0.06 wt%, and R-CDs@PVA 0.08 wt%, indicating the weight percentage composition of the R-CDs@PVA films. These samples were stored in a stability chamber maintained at 25 °C in darkness with a relative humidity of 75 %, and their structural and optical properties were monitored over time. Furthermore, the aqueous solution containing R-CDs@PVA NCs is drop-cast onto a mould, and subsequent solvent evaporation resulted in the formation of a flexible transparent film. The film is subsequently removed from the surface, uncovering a consistent fluorescent pattern exhibited on its surface. The schematic representation of fabrication of R-CDs@PVA NCs is depicted in Fig. 1 c.

To determine the QY, the standard reference sample Quinine sulfate (QS) in 0.1 M H2SO4 (QY of 54 %) is selected as the reference, making sure that, in the 10 mm FL cuvette, the absorption of the reference and R-CDs stayed below 0.10 in order to reduce the effects of reabsorption. The QY of the R-CDs sample is calculated using the following equation [39].
(1)

In the equation, QY and φstd stand for the FL QY of the R-CDs and the standard QS, respectively. Isample and Istd represent the integrated FL intensity of the R-CDs and the standard QS, while Asample and Astd indicate the absorbance values of the R-CDs and the standard QS, respectively. ηsample and ηstd denote the refractive indices of the sample and standard. Various R-CDs solutions and QS samples were prepared, ensuring that the absorption remained below 0.1 to minimize inner filter effects. To assess stability, prolonged and photo-bleaching experiments were carried out on the aqueous solution of R-CDs. FL emission spectra were recorded at various intervals to compare the FL intensity with that of the initial R-CDs solution, which is subsequently stored for 40 days to evaluate long-term stability. During the photo-bleaching experiments, FL emission is measured at different intervals while the sample is exposed to UV radiation (λexci= 365 nm). The solution is positioned at a distance of 100 mm from the UV light source, and the maximum FL intensity is kept constant. The FL intensity is monitored every 10 min over the 90 min treatment duration.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Coating of banana with PVA and R-CDs@PVA NC
ce:section.ce:para: Fresh bananas were obtained from the grocery store. These bananas were carefully selected to ensure uniformity in terms of size, colour, shape, texture, and decay before applying coatings or absence of noticeable marks. Initially, the bananas were washed and dried. Subsequently, the cleaned bananas are dipped twice in PVA and R-CDs@PVA (0.08 wt%) solution using the dip-coating method. Coated images of the bananas are captured using a Nikon camera over a period of 0–11 days for visual assessment.

The antioxidant capacity of R-CDs is evaluated using the DPPH (2, 2-diphenyl-1-picrylhydrazyl) assay, as described in previous studies [40]. To prepare 2.5 mL of the solution, R-CDs (0–100 μg) are mixed thoroughly with 50 % methanol. Subsequently, a dark room is incubated for 30 min to accommodate the 0.14 mM DPPH (140 μL) reagent. The control sample comprised 50 % methanol, and the optical density is assessed at 517 nm. The antioxidant property of R-CDs is determined using the following formula.

The blood samples are taken from healthy adults (ages 20–25) who are not smokers, drinkers, or drug users. Acid citrate dextrose (ACD) anticoagulant is made from dextrose, 71 mM citric acid, and 85 mM sodium citrate. After that, freshly drawn blood is added to a mixture in ACD and centrifuged for 10 min at 37 °C and 800 rpm. Following three rounds of washing RBCs with 10 mM PBS at pH 7.4, hematocrit (2 %) is generated by utilizing the washed RBCs for further investigations.

As previously described [41], the assessment of direct hemolytic activity is conducted. This involves combining 1 mL of human RBCs with 9 mL of 10 mM PBS (pH 7.4). This diluted blood sample (1 mL) is then treated with different doses of R-CDs (50–150 µg) for one hour at 37 °C. 9 mL of ice-cold, 10 mM PBS (pH 7.4) is added to halt the hemolytic process. Samples are centrifuged for 10 min at 1500 rpm at 37 °C. Quantification and comparison of the amount of hemoglobin generated in the sample supernatant at 540 nm are used to assess the degree of hemolysis.

To induce OS, the approach described in [42] is adopted. After treating 1 mL of freshly synthesized hematocrit (2 %) with 20 L of NaNO2, varied quantities of R-CDs (0–150 mg/mL) are applied. After being mixed, the solution is left to incubate at RT for a duration of 30 min. Following this incubation period, the reaction mixture is retrieved from the respective tubes at a concentration of 2 mg/mL. This allows for the investigation of how R-CDs influence OS markers. In the negative control, only RBCs are present without NaNO2, while in the positive control, only RBCs are present with NaNO2.

The methodology outlined in reference [43] is used to evaluate LPO. R-CDs (0–100 µg) are added to dry test tubes together with 1.5 mL of acetic acid (pH 3.5), 0.2 mL of 8 % SDS, and 1.5 mL of TBA (0.8 %). Two milligrams of protein from (2 % haematocrit obtained from erythrocytes lysate) are added to NaNO2. After 45 min of incubation at 45–60 °C, 3 mL of 1-butanol is added to the solutions. After that, the combination of TBARS (TBA reactive material) is centrifuged for 15 min at 5000 rpm, and the samples intensity is determined photometrically at 532 nm.

In accordance with procedure [44], DNPH is utilized to determine the PC content in the samples. NaNO2 (10 mM) and R-CDs (0–100 µg) are used to treat 1 mL of erythrocyte lysate (2 mg protein/mL). After adding 5 µL of 10 mM DNPH in 2 N HCl, these solutions are shaken frequently for an hour. In the control tubes, only 2 N HCl is introduced. Subsequent to the incubation period, the reaction mixtures underwent precipitation by the addition of 20 % TCA and are then centrifuged (5000 rpm/15 min). After a thorough cleaning with acetone, the precipitates are dissolved in 1 mL of 20 mM Tris-buffer (pH 7.4), 2 % SDS, and 0.14 mm NaCl. A 15 min centrifugation at 3600 rpm is performed on the precipitated materials. Using a molar absorptivity of 22 mM, the absorbance of the resultant protein solutions is determined. Mol carbonyl groups/mg protein is the unit of expression for the outcome.

The total thiols are evaluated using the procedure outlined in [45]. In brief, 1 mL of erythrocyte lysate, which contained 2 mg of protein/mL, is combined with 10 mM NaNO2 and 0–100 µg of R-CDs. After adding 1.975 mL of methanol, 0.375 mL of 0.2 M Tris-HCl buffer (pH 8.2), 10 mM of dithiol-bis-nitro benzoic acid (DTNB), and vertexes, the mixture is incubated for 30 min. After centrifuging the tubes for 10 min at 5000 rpm, the resulting clear supernatants are carefully collected. The thiol content, measured in nanomoles of DTNB oxidized per milligram of protein, is then determined. The spectroscopic absorbance at 412 nm served as the measurement parameter.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of CAT and SOD activities
ce:section.ce:para: The estimation of CAT and SOD activities are discussed elsewhere [46,47]. R-CDs ranging from 0 to 100 μg are incubated with erythrocytes (2 % haematocrit) and 10 mM NaNO2 for ∼ 2 h, after which they are lysed using DI water. The resulting lysate is employed in the SOD and CAT assays. For the SOD activity assay, 1 mL of a reaction mixture comprising 16 mM phosphate buffer (pH ∼7.8) and 8 mM/0.8 mM TEMED/EDTA is utilized. Subsequently, the reaction mixture is supplemented with 0.1 mL of erythrocyte lysate (0.05 mg of proteins), and the absorbance is measured at 406 nm for 1 min. In the CAT assay, 1 mL of 100 mM phosphate buffer (pH ∼7.4) containing 8.8 mM H2O2 is combined with the RBC lysate (0.05 mg of proteins). The CAT activity is determined by calculating the rate of H2O2 breakdown min−1mg−1 of protein. This is assessed by monitoring the decrease in absorbance at 240 nm over 3 min duration.

For the in vivo investigations, male Sprague Dawley rats are used as the animal model. The rats are bought from the Tumkur, Karnataka, India facilities of the Institution of Chromed Bioscience and kept in polypropylene (PP) cages with 6 rats in each cage. The rats used in the chosen experimental investigations are between 6 and 8 weeks old and weighed between 75 and 100 g. The experimental animals had unrestricted access to water and are fed pellets, the normal laboratory diet, as indicated. All enclosures are maintained at a temperature of 25 ± 3 °C with a humidity level of 55–65 %, and they followed a 12 h day light/12 h dark cycle, which is automated. According to the reference CBPL-IAEC-027–01/2023, the Institutional Animal Ethics Committee approved the study design and experimental procedure. Male Sprague Dawley rats weighing between 75 and 100 gm are divided into 7 groups, each consisting of 6 rats. The following protocol is followed as below:
Table
Group I Control (normal saline).
Group II Only Diclofenac
Group III
•
Initially Silymarin (25 mg kg−1 body wt/day) is injected intraperitonially.
•
After 45 min diclofenac (50 mg kg−1 body wt/day) is administered.
Group IV-VI
•
R-CDs (50, 100 and 150 mg kg−1 body wt/day for Group IV, V and VI) are inoculated intraperitonially.
•
After 45 min diclofenac (50 mgkg−1 body wt/day) is administered for Group III-VI.
Group VII R-CDs (150 mg kg−1 body weight/day) alone administered.

The rats were subjected to treatment over a period of 7 days. Following the administration of the final dose, they were instructed to fast for a duration of 12 h. The animals are then put to sleep with diethyl ether on the seventh day, and blood samples are taken via heart punctures. Using sterile voiles devoid of anticoagulant, 2 mL blood samples are extracted from the aorta for biochemical parameter analysis. Analysis is done on the biochemical parameters, which included alkaline phosphatase, globulin, albumin, total bilirubin (direct and indirect levels), SGPT and SGOT. Tissues from the liver, pancreas, kidney, and heart of the investigational animals are harvested and well-maintained in phosphate-buffered saline solution prior to homogenization for biochemical analysis. To enable histological investigation, the organs are additionally preserved in 10 % formalin.

The kidney, liver, pancreas and heart tissues from each group are prepared and paraffin surrounded for histological analysis. Sections ranging in thickness from 3 to 5 μm are stained with hematoxylin and eosin stains.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of R-LED and W-LED for plant growth
ce:section.ce:para: To fabricate the R-LED, 0 mg of R-CDs and 1.5 g of PVA were dissolved in ethanol. The mixture was then stirred at 80 °C for 15 min till the R-CDs were uniformly dispersed in the solution. Afterwards, the mixtures were applied onto the blue LED chips (450 nm) to produce the R-LED. For the W-LED, 10 mg of green commercial phosphors and 15 mg of R-CDs powder were combined with 3 g of PVA and stirred continuously to achieve a homogeneous and viscous mixture. At last, the mixture was applied onto the centre of the blue chip to create the W-LED.

Powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns are recorded in the range 10–80 ° using a Shimadzu 7000 instrument by employing CuKα (λ = 1.541 Å) radiation at tube voltage 36 kV and current 30 mA. The scanning rate and step are set at 2°/min and 0.02 °, respectively. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images are captured utilizing a JEM-2010 microscope operating at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV to investigate the morphology and dimensions of the R-CDs. The particle sizes of the R-CDs are evaluated utilizing the GATAN Microscopy Suite software, version 3.30 (Gatan, Inc., Pleasanton, CA, USA). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis is conducted using a Vacuum Generator (VG) ESCALAB MKII spectrometer, which is equipped with an AlK source and an XR4 dual anode. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) measurements are carried out employing a Nicolet FTIR-400 instrument with KBr pellets. XPS and FT-IR analyses are employed to ascertain the elements and chemical structures present in the R-CDs. Photoluminescence measurements are conducted using a Horiba (Jobin Yvon) spectrofluorometer, and FL decay curves are generated using a QM8000 FL spectrometer (Horiba Jobin Yvon Inc., Edison, NJ, USA). The QY of the R-CDs are determined using a QE-2100 quantum efficiency measurement system (Otsuka Electronics, Japan). To assess the photostability of the CNDs, 3 mL of the CND solution is dispensed into Petri dishes, which are then subjected to 365 nm UV light at 60 mW/cm2 in a darkened environment for up to 90 min.

The crystallinity and graphitization of the R-CDs are assessed via XRD analysis. The XRD examination revealed a prominent peak at around 21.2 ° (2θ), signifying the existence of the (002) lattice plane characteristic of carbon-based materials, with noticeable disordered carbon atoms apparent ( Fig. 2 a). The observed adequate degree of graphitization in the R-CDs could be attributed to the existence of nitrogen and oxygen-containing groups on their surface [48]. The partial graphitization of R-CDs is noticeable from their lowest peak at 2θ= 41.3 ° [49]. Calculations derived from the (002) lattice plane suggested an inter-layer spacing of 0.38 nm for the synthesized R-CDs, which is greater compared to bulk graphite (0.34 nm). This distinction is ascribed to the existence of functional groups, particularly nitrogen and oxygen, particularly along the edges of the R-CDs. Furthermore, the degree of graphitization, reflecting the carbon structure, is further elucidated using Raman spectroscopy. The Raman spectrum exhibited approximately comparable intensities of the D and G bands (Fig. 2 b), indicating a similar number of defects alongside graphitic carbon [50]. The D band at 1365 cm−1 indicates the presence of sp3 defects and a turbostratic carbon arrangement in the synthesized structure, while the G band at 1587 cm−1 signifies the sp2 order of carbon [51]. The ID/IG ratio calculated from the Raman spectra, approximately 1 (0.92), implies that the R-CDs contain surface/edge defects of functional groups. To further verify the presence of functional groups in CDs, the FTIR spectroscopy revealed stretching vibrations of N-H and O-H at 3434 cm−1, as depicted in Fig. 2 c. This indicates the presence of functional hydroxyl and amino groups in the CDs, which may contribute to their excellent solubility in water. The absorption bands detected at 1639 cm−1, 1384 cm−1, 1113 cm−1, and 620 cm−1 signify stretching vibrations originating from functional groups associated with CN, C-NH, C-O, and CS, respectively [52]. The morphology of the R-CDs is scrutinized through TEM imaging (Fig. 2 d). Upon examination with TEM, lattice fringes are observed with an interplanar spacing of 0.22 nm. These lattice fringes closely resemble the (100) diffraction facets typically found in graphite carbon. This observation strongly indicates that the synthesized R-CDs possess an amorphous structure. The quasi-spherical R-CDs displayed a size distribution ranging from ∼1–9 nm, with a predominant peak observed around 4.4 nm. This sizing analysis is conducted by counting 70 particles, as depicted in Fig. 2 e.

Fig. 2 f illustrates XPS spectra can be utilized for collecting additional information about the functional groups on the surface of R-CDs. The elements carbon (C), oxygen (O), and nitrogen (N) were found in the R-CDs, as shown by the XPS spectra. These elements corresponded to peaks seen at 284.78, 530.58 and 284.78 eV, respectively [53]. The high-resolution C 1 s spectrum (Fig. 2 g) validates the existence of two peaks at 284.78 and 287.25 eV, corresponding to C–C/CC and CO/CN, respectively [54]. The O 1 s spectrum (Fig. 2 h) exhibits two peaks related to CO (530.58 eV) and C–OH/C–O–C (531.16 eV), suggesting the presence of oxygen-rich groups [55]. Upon examination of the N 1 s spectrum (Fig. 2 i), two peaks are discerned, which are associated with amine N (398.31 eV) and graphitic N (399.14 eV). These peaks are attributed to pyrrolic C2-N-H and graphitic N-C3, respectively [56]. As mentioned earlier, the R-CDs are composed of extensive π-conjugated domains and demonstrate the formation of polyaromatic structures.

The R-CDs optical properties are examined using FL and ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) spectrophotometry. The π-π* transition of CC in the sp2 region is responsible for the prominent absorbance peak at 238 nm in the UV–vis absorption spectra seen in Fig. 3 a [57]. Moreover, the wide absorption band spanning approximately 450–550 nm indicates the n-π* transition of CN and CO bonds [58]. Additionally, the FL spectra of the R-CDs demonstrate a peak emission at 625 nm, with a maximum excitation wavelength of 520 nm (Fig. 3 a). The R-CDs solution exhibited a light brown colour under daylight and emitted red FL when excited at 365 nm (inset of Fig. 3 a). To investigate the FL emission of R-CDs in more detail, the chromaticity diagram (CIE) is employed (Fig. 3 b). The calculated CIE coordinates of 0.6915 and 0.3080 confirm the synthesized R-CDs' considerable potential as a FL probe in biological systems. As the excitation wavelength shifts gradually from 470 nm to 550 nm, the FL emission intensity of R-CDs exhibits an initial increase followed by a decrease. Nevertheless, the peak emission value remains consistent ( Fig. 3 c), indicating that the emission peak at 625 nm is inherent to the structure of R-CDs. The high QY of R-CDs enabled the distinct visualization of deep red emission under 470–550 nm excitation ( Fig. 3 d). Furthermore, the emitted colour coordinates are assessed using a chromaticity diagram. The CIE 1931 plot illustrating the colour coordinates of photoluminescence for the prepared samples is depicted in Fig. 3 e. A study on the lifetime of R-CDs is carried out to investigate their optical features in more detail. A tri-exponential function, which is described in detail below, provides a good fit for the FL decay curve of R-CDs [59].
(2)
