Three separate decay lifetimes (T1, T2, and T3) make up the PL decay function Y(t), which fluctuates with time. A1, A2, and A3 reflect the fractional contributions of each decay lifetime. The decay curve is depicted in Fig. 3 f, with detailed information on various degradation zones shown in inset of Fig. 3 f. Three distinct emissive sites may exist on the R-CDs, based on the exponential behaviour that has been seen. T2 and T3 denote longer lives, suggesting the protracted relaxation of excitons to ground energy levels, while T1 represents the lifetime of direct electron-hole recombination. Three different emission sites with average lifetimes of (2.483 ± 2.725), (2.484 ± 3.300), and (11.345 ± 1.876) ns are identified by the extraordinary fit of the tri-exponential curve (Rtri 2 = 0.999 > Rbi 2 = 0.998) to the time-resolved PL decaying curves. The average lifetime of R-CDs is determined to be 6.79 ns, aligning with values documented in existing literature. In order to gain deeper insights into the FL characteristics of R-CDs, we systematically adjusted the excitation wavelength within the range of 270–350 nm, with increments of 10 nm. At an excitation wavelength (λexci) of 520 nm, we observed a maximum emission (λemi) of 625 nm, as illustrated in Fig. 3 g & h. Additionally, the QY of R-CDs is assessed to be 16.5 % in an aqueous medium at RT, with QS serving as the reference (λexci = 370 nm, QY = 54 %).

While various analytical techniques exist for determining metal levels, employing R-CDs as a sensing probe offers a straightforward and cost-effective approach. Fig. 3 i illustrates the PL spectra of blank R-CDs alongside different metal ions (Cr3+, Zn2+, Hg2+, Fe2+, Ni2+, Fe3+, Cu2+, Pb2+, Co2+ and Cd2+), allowing for the examination of FL quenching properties. Upon examining the photoluminescence (PL) spectra, it becomes apparent that the R-CDs solution devoid of any metal ions displays its highest emission intensity at 625 nm. However, upon introducing different metal ions, there is a noticeable decrease in FL intensity. With the introduction of various metal ions, a clear phenomenon of FL quenching is observed, characterized by a decrease in emission intensity. The Stern-Volmer equation is a fundamental relationship that is used to describe the quenching in FL intensity when a quencher is present. The analysis showed that the most substantial quenching effect occurs when Fe3+ ions are added to the R-CDs solution (Fig. 3 j), as expressed in the following equation [60]:
(3)
F
F
0
= 1 +
K
SV
( Q )

KSV is the Stern-Volmer quenching constant, Q is the quencher concentration, and F0 is the FL intensity in the absence of the quencher and F is the FL intensity in the presence of the quencher. This equation describes the linear relationship between the FL intensity ratio (F0/F) and the concentration of the quencher [61]. By plotting (F0/F) against Q, one can determine the quenching constant KSV and gain insights into the quenching mechanism and the interactions between the fluorophore and the quencher. The quenching tendency is shown for Cd2+, Co2+, Cr3+, Zn2+, Hg2+, Pb2+, Ni2+, Cu2+, Fe2+, and Fe3+, with the Cd3+ ions showing the least amount of quenching. The charge-selective and ion-specific characteristics of the functional groups on the surface of R-CDs are responsible for the enhanced selectivity for Fe3+. The R-CDs surface oxygen groups interact with Fe3+ to produce complexes that alter the surface energy trap distribution. The non-radiative assembly of electrons and holes is enhanced by this interaction, leading to the quenching of FL. The sensitivity of R-CDs in detecting Fe3+ ions is assessed by examining the correlation between the FL quenching of R-CDs and the varying Fe3+ ions concentrations, ranging from 8×10−8 M to 2.4×10−5 M. Fig. 3 k depicts the FL spectra of R-CDs when exposed to various Fe3+ ions concentrations, on the other hand, Fig. 3 l shows the linear relationship between F0/F and the concentration of Fe3+ ions. It is clearly seen that R-CD FL intensity decreases with increasing Fe3+ ion concentration between 8×10−8 M and 2.4×10−5 M. The FL spectra provide a visual representation of how the FL intensity decreases as the concentration of Fe3+ ions increase. The mechanism behind FL quenching in the presence of Fe3+ ions involve electron transfer between the R-CDs and Fe3+ ions. A robust linear correlation is observed across the concentration range from 8×10−8 M to 2.4 ×10−5 M (Regression equation: y = 0.5529x + 0.00981, R2 = 0.9815). The limit of detection (LOD) is determined utilizing the respective equation [62].
(4)
LOD =
3 σ
s

Here, σ represents the standard deviation value, and s denotes the slope of the linear fit. The computed limit of detection (LOD) for the synthesized R-CDs is 0.53 μM, a value consistent with those reported in prior literature ( Table 2 [63−87]. The FL intensity of the synthesized R-CDs is analyzed at various pH levels, as depicted in Fig. 4 a. It is noted that the synthesized R-CDs exhibit sensitivity to pH, with the highest FL intensity observed at pH ∼7. The protonation and deprotonation of functional groups explain variations in emission intensity under basic and acidic environments. Intramolecular hydrogen bonding results from functional groups being protonated at lower pH levels. Aggregation of R-CDs occurs due to intramolecular hydrogen bonding at lower pH, leading to a reduction in FL intensity. Conversely, at higher pH, deprotonated functional groups generate negative charges, forming an anionic layer on the R-CDs surface. The FL origin is disturbed by this, leading to a decrease in emission intensity. The consistent and maximum emission intensity observed within the neutral pH range highlights the potential of the synthesized R-CDs as probes for imaging and diverse biological applications. Fig. 4 b illustrates the normalized emission intensities of the CDs, showing a gradual increase in intensity until reaching pH 7. Subsequently, it begins to decrease as the pH rises towards 14. Photos of the R-CDs solution under UV 365 nm excitation at various pH values (1, 3, 5, 7, 9, and 14) are depicted in Fig. 4 c. It is evident from the images that the R-CDs solution exhibits pH sensitivity. Over the course of 0–48 h under visible light, the hues of the R-CDs solution remained consistent with the original R-CDs solution at pH 7, 8, and 9. However, the solutions colour at pH 10, 11, 12, and 13 appeared as a faint yellow after 0 h. As the pH increased, the hue of the R-CDs solution darkened notably at pH 1, 3, 9 and 14. Interestingly, despite the fading colour of the solution, the FL intensity at each pH remained largely unchanged. This indicates the system's capability to consistently and reliably monitor pH values over time.

We conducted additional tests to assess the resistance of the R-CDs to photobleaching by exposing them to continuous irradiation under an ultraviolet lamp for a duration of 0–50 min [88]. Remarkably, the emission intensity of the synthesized R-CDs showed no discernible change throughout the duration of the experiment, as illustrated in Fig. 4 d. Fig. 4 e & f show the R-CDs solution's normalized intensity and digital images, respectively. As shown, the R-CDs exhibit a little drop in FL intensity, suggesting that they are suitable for long-term storage and use and that they have a strong resistance to photobleaching. Fig. 4 g & h depict how the FL intensity of R-CDs is monitored across various aging time intervals to assess their fluorescent stability or photostability. It is remarkable that the FL intensity of PCDs does not change significantly from day 1 to day 40 of aging [89]. It suggests that the structure of R-CDs is extremely resistant to environmental influences because photo-bleaching does not occur even after 40 days. FL digital photos, depicted in the inset of Fig. 4 i, further corroborate the stability of R-CDs FL. Aqueous solutions of R-CDs maintain a consistent, vibrant red colour emission visible under a 365 nm UV lamp from day 1 to day 40, showcasing the sustained FL stability of R-CDs over an extended duration.

Fig. 5 illustrates the quenching and subsequent re-quenching phenomena observed in R-CDs. Upon the introduction of 15 μM ascorbic acid (Cd2+), the FL of the R-CDs is promptly quenched again. Interestingly, the R-CDs solution transitions to a blue hue instead of black, indicating the resurgence of FL. The reappearance of FL may be linked to the release of loosely bound Fe3+ from the surface of the R-CDs, facilitated by the strong interaction between Cd2+ and Fe3+. The functionality of logic gates (LG) is assessed through ON-OFF experiments using the FL quenching and re-quenching technique [90]. The FL ON-OFF assay of CDs sourced from different origins is outlined in Fig. 5 a. This highlights how AC ions electrostatically coordinate with and engage with CDs, resulting in the aggregation of Cd2+ ions, forming interconnected amorphous masses that induce heightened FL emission (Fig. 5 b). These observations are particularly significant, demonstrating the remarkable selectivity of R-CDs for Cd2+ and Fe3+ ions, along with their dual FL response characteristics, in their initial synthesized state.

Logical gates (LGs) serve as essential elements within logical circuits, receiving inputs and producing corresponding outputs through specific operations [91]. In this study, the capacity of the R-CDs solution to diminish FL upon the introduction of Fe3+ and its subsequent restoration following the addition of Cd2+ are utilized for this purpose. To evaluate the quenching and re-quenching tendencies of R-CDs under diverse input conditions, the NOT and IMPLICATION LGsare utilized. The NOT gate reverses its input and output to function as a logical negation or inverter. Here, input 1 is associated with the presence of Fe3+ and causes FL quenching of the CDs, while input 0 is associated with the lack of Fe3+. Based on this information, the truth table and LG diagram are created. Two assertions are shown to be logically related using the IMPLY gate. In this instance, Fe3+ and Cd2+ are used as input states '1' and their absence as '0' in the construction of the implied LG. The output for every input condition is recorded, demonstrating how the synthesized R-CDs may be used to develop more advanced molecular logic systems. In Fig. 5 c, the NOT LG is illustrated alongside its respective truth table. The functionality of the NOT gate relies on the quenching of FL in CDs. Here, input 1 represents the presence of Fe3+, while input 0 indicates its absence. The R-CDs solution reaches its peak intensity when Fe3+ is not present, indicating an output of 1 (ON state). Conversely, no intensity is seen when Fe3+ is present, suggesting an output of 0 (OFF state). This information is utilized to construct precise truth tables and LGs. The digital LG IMPLY logically connects two propositions, with one input passing through a NOT gate and the other through an OR gate [92]. Using this fundamental understanding, the implication LG is formulated and its functionality evaluated. The complementary output produced by the NOT gate is directed into the second input of the OR gate. As a result, it is anticipated that the resulting output of the IMPLY gate will mimic the output of the OR gate. The IMPLICATION's functionality LG and its corresponding truth table are dependent on Fe3+ and Cd2+ sensitive switching technology. This is depicted in Fig. 5 d. The components Fe3+ and Cd2+ signify the state '1', whereas their absence indicates the state '0'. The output is observed to exhibit maximum FL when neither Fe3+ nor Cd2+ (0/0 state) are present. Each input condition is described. This results in an output of 1 (ON state). When Cd2+ is absent and Fe3+ (0/1) is the input condition, no FL is observed, yielding an output of 0 (OFF state). The R-CDs solution exhibits FL when Cd2+ is present without Fe3+ (1/0) as the input state, resulting in an output of 1 (ON state). When both Cd2+ and Fe3+ are present, FL and an output of 1 (ON state) are generated. These results highlight the potential of synthesized R-CDs in the development of the next generation of molecular logic circuits [93].

In practical applications, transparency holds significant importance, especially in UV-blocking agents or films. With this consideration in mind, we developed ultra-transparent, flexible, and thin UV-blocking R-CDs@PVA NCs. This achievement involves incorporating fluorescent R-CDs into a biocompatible and biodegradable polymer, PVA. Photographic images presented in Fig. 6 a depict the transparency of PVA film and R-CDs@PVA NCs at varying concentrations (0.02, 0.04, 0.06 and 0.08 wt%) of R-CDs under both daylight and UV 365 nm light. These images demonstrate that the composite films maintain high transparency under visible light, with minimal impact on transparency even as the concentration of R-CDs@PVA NCs increases up to 0.08 wt%. Furthermore, various deformation tests are conducted as depicted in Fig. 6 b. Remarkably, regardless of bending, rolling, twisting, or folding (including single, double, and multiple folding), minimal structural or fluorescent defects are observed on the surface of the R-CDs@PVA NCs. Moreover, the R-CDs@PVA NCs exhibits the ability to conform perfectly to surfaces of diverse shapes, including origami, cuboid, cylinder, and wrinkled surfaces. This characteristic underscores its suitability for a wide range of complex scenarios, showcasing its outstanding flexibility. These observations indicate that the resulting film demonstrates excellent mechanical properties and maintains stable FL even when subjected to various forms of deformation. This resilience is crucial for its application in wearable fluorescent devices.

The endurance of photoluminescence demonstrated by R-CDs@PVA NCs is a matter of considerable interest for substantiated claims. Several factors, including aging, UV irradiation, mechanical deformation, pH effects, and stretching under loads, can impact their performance. To investigate this, the study initially examined the FL emission intensity of R-CDs@PVA NCs over different aging durations. The stability examination of R-CDs@PVA NCs indicates negligible alteration in FL intensity throughout the 60 days storage period, as illustrated in Fig. 6 c. This outcome implies that the R-CDs retained stability over the entire duration, exhibiting minimal fluctuations. To assess the resilience of the R-CDs@PVA NCs emission against photoinduced degradation, R-CDs@PVA NCs films are subjected to continuous UV irradiation for various durations ranging from 0 to 60 min. For this experiment, R-CDs@PVA (0.08 wt%) NCs were selected due to their faster reaction rate and higher yield. Upon UV exposure, minimal changes in normalized intensity were observed even after 30 min of UV irradiation (Fig. 6 d). The findings suggest that R-CDs@PVA (0.08 wt%) NCs exhibit high resistance to photo bleaching. To assess their mechanical stability, the samples were subjected to mechanical loads ranging from 0 to 5 tons for 30 min each (Fig. 6 e). Upon the alleviation of mechanical strain, the films exhibited uniform performance under both standard lighting conditions and UV 365 nm light. This highlights the exceptional mechanical durability of the produced films, making them well-suited for utilization in traffic signalling and solid-state lighting applications. Additionally, as the illustration shows, FL analyses are performed to evaluate the distribution of FL intensity within the flexible films that generate orange-red light at varying mechanical strains ranging from 0 to 5 tons. A homogeneous distribution of PL intensity in the films is revealed by FL mapping within the black dot region (Fig. 6 e) using an excitation wavelength of 320 nm. These findings imply potential applications of the films in flexible display devices. Flexibility is a critical aspect for their usability in various applications. To investigate this further, the study initially assessed the FL emission intensity of R-CDs@PVA (0.08 wt%) NCs in different aqueous environments with varying pH levels. The FL intensity remained largely unaffected across a range of pH values (Fig. 6 f), consistent with previous findings. Fig. 6 f illustrates the relationship between the FL intensity of R-CDs@PVA (0.08 wt%) NCs and pH levels. The synthesized R-CDs@PVA (0.08 wt%) NCs demonstrate stability across the pH range of 1–14, making them suitable for diverse applications. Moreover, the tensile test outcomes depicted in Fig. 6 g confirm the commendable mechanical properties of R-CDs@PVA (0.08 wt%) NCs, exhibiting a tensile strength of up to 71.49 MPa. Notably, even after subjecting the film to a load of 1000 gm, the photoluminescence intensity of the film remained nearly unchanged.

Additional analysis is conducted to assess the shape recovery capabilities of R-CDs @PVA (0.08 wt%) NCs, as demonstrated in Fig. 7 a-d, where a quatrefoil-shaped specimen is fabricated and subjected to deformation by folding. It is evident that each petal of the closed flower shape efficiently expanded through the EDTA, water, heat, and glucose-induced recovery process within approximately 300 sec [94]. This illustrates the remarkable shape memory property of the R-CDs@PVA (0.08 wt%) NCs. Crucially, the entire recovery procedure can be monitored under UV 365 nm lamp due to the vibrant red FL emitted by R-CDs @PVA (0.08 wt%) NCs. Currently, polymer materials endowed with adjustable shape memory properties hold significant promise in biomedical applications, tissue engineering, and beyond. In our prior research, we demonstrated the remarkable shape memory effect of R-CDs@PVA (0.08 wt percent) NCs produced by the co-precipitation technique when it came to water. Expanding on the pH sensitivity demonstrated by R-CDs@PVA (0.08 wt%) NCs, we looked at how pH levels affected the shape memory effect caused by water [95]. Fig. 7 e-g illustrates the shape recovery process of R-CDs@PVA (0.08 wt%) NCs at various pH levels. Remarkably, the R-CDs@PVA (0.08 wt%) NCs demonstrated remarkable shape recovery in neutral, acidic and alkaline environments, achieving recovery rates of 95 %, 97 % and 91 %, respectively. In contrast, the samples exhibited different rates of recovery at distinct pH levels. For instance, at pH = 1, the recovery rates approached approximately 97 % after 60 sec of immersion, while during the same period, the sample immersed in an alkaline environment displayed only a 21 % recovery rate. In the pH = 1 environment, the recovery rate is notably rapid, with nearly complete shape restoration achieved within 30 min. Conversely, the pH = 13 environment exhibited the slowest shape recovery (Fig. 7 h). These discrepancies in shape memory effects across different pH values can be attributed to their sensitivity to pH variations. Under neutral conditions, the presence of infiltrated water acts as a plasticizer, disrupting certain hydrogen bonds. This disruption increases chain mobility within the amorphous region, satisfying the reversible phase criteria. Conversely, in acidic conditions, the diffusion of a substantial quantity of H+ ions result in the breakdown of hydrogen bonds formed by amino and hydroxyl groups [96]. Consequently, this process further diminishes the hydrogen bonding network. This process enables the release of initially trapped stress, particularly when the glass transition temperature is lower than RT, leading to a phenomenon of shape recovery. The reduction in physical cross-linking points accelerates the diffusion of water, thereby enhancing the rapidity of shape recovery behaviour. On the contrary, in an alkaline environment with minimal H+ concentration, the hydrogen bond network retains higher stability, hindering water diffusion and requiring a prolonged period for shape recovery. Nevertheless, the shape recovery of undiluted PVA film post-deformation faced notable challenges, yielding a recovery ratio of merely 29.3 %, even following a 120 sec immersion in water with a pH of 7 ( Fig. 7 i). These findings highlight the superiority of R-CDs@PVA (0.08 wt%) NCs over pure PVA concerning the shape memory effect.

Yet another significant application of this R-CDs@PVA film lies in its role as an effective UV shield while maintaining excellent transparency to visible light. The spectral transmittance of single and double layers of R-CDs@PVA film, created using an R-CDs concentration of 0.06 wt%, reveals that a lone layer of R-CDs@PVA film (∼40 μm) efficiently absorbs most of the detrimental UV-A light while enabling approximately 94 % transmission of the visible light component ( Fig. 8 a). Nevertheless, achieving complete UV-A blocking with just a single 40 µm layer of this film is not feasible. Increasing the R-CD concentration to 0.08 wt% in PVA effectively blocks UV-A entirely, but it results in diminished visible light transparency (∼75 %). To overcome this constraint and achieve both excellent transparency and comprehensive UV-A blocking, another layer of R-CDs@PVA NCs (∼40 μm) is added on top of the initial layer. This dual-layer setup of R-CDs@PVA NCs retained comparable transparency to the single-layer film in visible light, while effectively obstructing UV-A light entirely (Fig. 8 a). Such outstanding attributes render our R-CDs@PVA NCs exceptionally suitable for a wide array of practical applications that demand cost-effective, biocompatible, transparent, UV-protective plastics. A significant prospective application could be within greenhouse crop cultivation setups (Fig. 8 b). The capability to obstruct UV light in greenhouses provides notable benefits, including protecting plants from detrimental UV radiation while permitting the passage of essential visible light necessary for photosynthesis and plant development. Comprehensive UV-A blocking additionally offers defence against insect pests such as aphids, whiteflies and thrips, which are drawn to UV-A and have the potential to harm crops. Thus, the use of R-CDs@PVA NCs for complete UV-A blocking could offer significant advantages for greenhouse agriculture. Additionally, R-CDs are known for efficiently converting UV light into blue light, further enhancing their suitability for this application. When a CD solution is directly exposed to a UV LED torch, strong red luminescence is noticeable (Fig. 8 c: d I). However, when a R-CDs@PVA film is placed between the CD solution (0.02–0.08 wt%) and the UV torch (Fig. 8 d II-VI), only faint red luminescence is observed. When a second layer of R-CDs@PVA (0.08 wt%) is added, the residual UV light is entirely absorbed, resulting in no luminescence from the CD solution (Fig. 8 d: VI). The schematic in Fig. 13 a illustrates the mechanism of R-CDs in the application of UV-blocking film. This simple experiment demonstrates the effectiveness of the R-CDs@PVA NCs UV-blocking capabilities and suggests that they are suitable for a variety of UV-protective applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: UV shielding behaviour of the R-CDs@PVA NCs
ce:section.ce:para: In Fig. 9 A, a straightforward experimental configuration is presented to assess the R-CDs NCs ability to shield UV light. Directly beneath the polymer film holder, at a distance of 3 cm, is a UV digital lux meter [97]. Two alternatives for the light source were natural daylight and a UV lamp. The UV light intensity passed through the polymer film is measured with a digital lux UV meter. Subsequently, films with escalating R-CDs content are evaluated under both lighting conditions, and the outcomes are depicted in Fig. 9 a-f. Notably, a pure PVA polymer film devoid of R-CDs shields ∼7.57 % of UV-B light. The polymer film representative of 0.02 wt% R-CDs exhibited a UV-blocking capability of approximately 36.86 % under UV lamp illumination. Similarly, UV light intensities were absorbed by additional polymer films with R-CD concentrations of 0.04 wt%, 0.06 wt%, and 0.08 wt%, which were roughly 51.86 %, 65.86 %, and 74.86 %, respectively, as depicted in Fig. 9 b. A similar experiment conducted under daylight (UV-A) yielded consistent results, as depicted in Fig. 9 a. These findings led to the conclusion that an optimal concentration of 0.08 wt% R-CDs@PVA NCs provides superior UV shielding capability. This conclusion is supported by the UV–vis absorption spectrum spanning from 0.02 to 0.08 wt%, illustrated in Fig. 9 b. Notably, the UV light shielding effectiveness of the 0.08 wt%R-CDs@PVA NCs is confirmed by the presence of a pronounced absorption peak within the UV spectral region. Similarly, the experiment conducted under 254 nm light (UV-C) yielded consistent results, as depicted in Fig. 9 c.

To improve UV shielding effectiveness, we prepared various thick polymer films using a dispersed polymer solution containing 0.08 wt% R-CDs (8, 10, 12, and 14 mL). These NCs are tested for UV shielding effectiveness with increasing thickness under both UV lamp and daytime conditions, as indicated in Fig. 9 d-f. Direct daylight (UV-A) intensity is recorded at 3200 μW/cm2. It is remarkable that, as shown in Fig. 9 d, the 100 μm (8 mL) thick polymer layer with 0.08 wt% R-CDs absorbs about 84.37 % (500 μW/cm2). On the other hand, PVA polymer film of the same thickness absorbs about 11.91 % (2819 μW/cm2) of UV radiation, while the 250 μm (14 mL) thick PVA polymer film exhibits the highest UV shielding capability of 21.31 % in the daylight (UV-A), as shown in Fig. 9 g. This outcome underscores the significance of the UV shielding properties conferred by R-CDs. Consequently, the trend of UV-A light absorption is observed to increase with the thickness of the polymer films. For instance, R-CDs@PVA in a 150 μm (10 mL) thick polymer film absorbed approximately 88.66 % (363 μW/cm2) of UV-A light. Nonetheless, a noteworthy enhancement is noted in the R-CDs@PVA polymer film, measuring 250 μm (14 mL) in thickness, attaining a maximum UV-A light shielding of almost 97.34 % (85 μW/cm2) in bright daylight ( Fig. 9 d). Furthermore, as illustrated in Fig. 9 e and f, the UV light shielding capacity of various thick polymer films (R-CDs@PVA in 100, 150, 200, and 250 μm) is assessed using a UV lamp (UV-B and UV-C). Fig. 9 h illustrates the immaculate PVA polymer film with the same thickness had a UV-B light shielding capability of about ∼21.14 % (552 μW/cm2) for 250 μm (14 mL). However, the polymer films with thicknesses of 150 μm and 250 μm shielded approximately ∼87.71 % (87 μW/cm2) and 96.71 % (23 μW/cm2), respectively ( Fig. 9 e). Similarly, the experiment conducted under 254 nm light (UV-C) for different thick polymer films (R-CDs@PVA in 100, 150, 200, and 250 μm) (Fig. 9 f) and different thick polymer PVA film (PVA in 100, 150, 200, and 250 μm) yielded consistent results, as depicted in Fig. 9 i. Conversely, the same thickness PVA polymer film absorbs approximately 21.31 % (2518 μW/cm2) of UV-A radiation, whereas the greatest UV shielding capacity of 24.62 % is observed in the daylight (UV-C) for the 250 μm (14 mL) thick PVA polymer film, as shown in Fig. 9 i. These results show that the UV shielding capacity of the polymer film increases dramatically with thickness, regardless of the light source [98]. This highlights how important film thickness is to improving the film's UV shielding properties. We obtained quantitative measurements of the polymer film's UV shielding capabilities using a UV digital lux meter. The considerable light scattering and reflection made possible by CDs is responsible for the film's enhanced UV shielding capacity. This process efficiently transforms UV photon energy into heat. Quantification of the UV shielding capacity of R-CDs@PVA (250 μm) dispersed polymer films and pristine PVA yields values of around 96.69 % and 96.76 %, respectively. The significant increase in UV light shielding more than 86.13 % that was seen in the R-CDs distributed polymer films can be ascribed to the CDs surface modification, which makes it easier for them to disperse throughout the polymer matrix. As a result, it is determined that the 0.08 wt% of R-CDs dispersed polymer films might provide a better substitute for protecting UV light-sensitive objects.
Tables 3–5 lists the transmittance values for various concentrations, R-CDs@PVA NCs thicknesses, and pure PVA film thicknesses in the UV-A, UV-B, and UV-C areas.

To elucidate its potential in the printing technology, R-CDs@PVA NCs are utilized as the ink-free pattern substrate for creating the pattern "GANESHA," as depicted in Fig. 10 a. The relief template is coated with R-CDs@PVA NCs, and after solvent evaporation, a transparent film is fabricated, which is then peeled off from the mould. The resulting film exhibited fluorescent "GANESHA" patterns, with intense FL is observed. The relief templates are shown in Fig. 10 b and c in both UV 365 nm and daytime conditions. R-CDs flowed toward the elevated regions as the solvent evaporated, propelled by the external capillary flow-induced coffee ring effect [99], as seen in Fig. 10 d. Additionally, driven by ordering based on entropy in the system of R-CDs@PVA NCs, R-CDs have a propensity to move in the direction of the film-substrate interface. As a result, R-CDs segregate toward the interface between the patterned or embossed area and the R-CDs@PVA NCs when they are put onto relief templates. We generated "GANESHA" patterns on R-CDs@PVA NCs using this viewing mechanism (Fig. 10 e & f). Similarly, this inkless printing technology can effectively transfer and visualize the relief designs. After the ink-free printing process, we carried out additional experiments to monitor the FL variations of R-CDs@PVA NCs under various environmental conditions (alkaline solution and UV irradiation), leveraging the multi-stimuli responsive characteristics, including shape memory and FL properties (as illustrated in Fig. 10 g & h). It was found that when subjected to UV irradiation or alkaline solutions, the fluorescent patterns created by R-CDs@PVA NCs remained unaltered. It became clear that even though the films were recovering their shape, the surface patterns FL intensity was stable when exposed to UV light. The R-CDs@PVA NCs synthesized in this investigation demonstrate multifunctionality, presenting a novel avenue for designing and manufacturing advanced, comprehensive AC and information encryption solutions. These materials show potential for use in demanding environments characterized by high alkalinity and UV irradiation.

Furthermore, fluorescent R-CDs are examined as potential anticounterfeiting markers for the development of security inks. Counterfeiting is an arena where fluorescent R-CDs can be utilized effectively, aiding in the differentiation between authentic and counterfeit items. Given that these CD systems exhibit FL solely under UV excitation, they are suitable for counterfeit detection purposes [100]. The appropriate amount of R-CDs is mixed with the PVA solution to create the security inks. The viscosity of these security inks can be modified by changing the PVA solution's weight percentage. This adjustment allows for precise control over the flow and adherence properties of the ink, optimizing its performance for various printing processes and substrates. Fig. 11 a & b illustrate photographs of the AC labels produced using viscous inks derived from the fabricated optimized R-CDs, observed under UV 365 nm excitation and daylight, respectively. Under natural daylight, it is evident that there is no FL observed. However, when visualized under 365 nm UV excitation, the AC patterns display a vivid red emission attributed to the R-CDs security ink. This strong FL emission from R-CDs serves as evidence of the promising application of R-CDs in security inks [101,102].

When evaluating FL stability, we compare the hand-drawn image with its stored counterpart over an extended duration. Notably, even after 45 days of storage (Fig. 11 c), the FL intensity of the hand-drawn image exhibits minimal alteration, affirming the remarkable FL stability of R-CDs. This durability underscores the potential longevity and reliability of R-CDs for various applications, including AC measures and information encryption. Additionally, the resulting security ink possesses transparency, eco-friendliness, durability, biocompatibility, and easy washability [103]. These attributes make the aqueous solution of R-CDs an attractive option for fluorescent sketch pens. The combination of these qualities not only ensures a pleasant user experience but also enhances the versatility and sustainability of such writing instruments. In natural daylight, handwritten text and hand-drawn images appear colourless or barely visible, underscoring the potential of R-CDs for applications in information encryption and AC measures. This property enhances the security and authenticity of documents and products, as the fluorescent characteristics of R-CDs become apparent only under specific lighting conditions, providing a covert layer of protection against unauthorized duplication or tampering [104,105]. Continuing our investigation into practical characteristics, we extensively assess the photo-stability of R-CDs. As depicted in Fig. 11 d, the AC patterns applied on paper substrate are subjected to an intense UV lamp for varying durations (5–60 min). The CD intensities remain nearly unchanged throughout the exposure period of 0–60 min, demonstrating their exceptional photostability. In order to assess the AC patterns ability to withstand temperature changes, we examine patterns created on marble surfaces and subject them to temperatures between 50 and 80 °C for 30 min time duration (Fig. 11 e). This investigation aims to gauge the ability of the patterns to maintain their integrity and visibility under conditions of heat stress, providing valuable insights into their suitability for real-world applications where temperature fluctuations may occur. The findings unequivocally demonstrate that the patterns maintain their clarity and reliability even at temperatures as high as 80 °C.

On the contrary, delving into the realm of multiple information encryption and decryption presented an intriguing research direction. We synthesized "on-off-on" R-CDs and successfully realized the multi-encryption of information using FL technology. Initially, the encrypted information (in the form of a flower) is inscribed on the filter paper using R-CDs FL ink, rendering it imperceptible under daylight conditions ( Fig. 12 a). The encrypted contents are initially decrypted under a 365 nm ultraviolet lamp (Fig. 12 b). Following this, the filter paper is submerged in a beaker filled with Fe3+ solution for a period of 1 min. No visible information is discernible under sunlight or UV lamp, affirming the effective encryption of information utilizing the R-CDs-Fe3+ system (Fig. 12 c). Ultimately, the filter paper containing R-CDs-Fe3+ is introduced into a test tube with Cd2+ for varying durations. Subsequently, the FL of the R-CDs is restored and became visualized under UV 365 nm light, signifying the successful decryption of information by the system (Fig. 12 d). Hence, R-CDs can serve as FL ink for writing and painting, and they can also be employed for information storage, encryption, and various commercial applications owing to their unique fluorescent characteristics [106].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Food packaging using dip coating method
ce:section.ce:para: To begin, the bananas undergo washing and drying procedures. Next, the washed bananas are coated twice with PVA solution, followed by application of R-CDs@PVA solution using the dip-coating technique [107]. The coated and untreated bananas are then dried by air at RT (25 °C) using a specially made apparatus. For visual evaluation, pictures of the bananas are taken using a camera during a period of 0–12 days ( Fig. 13 a). The experiment is conducted with a minimum of four repetitions. Bananas, a typical example of perishable fruit, function as the test model to determine whether R-CDs@PVA-based films are effective at extending the shelf life of food. The bananas pre- and post-storage changes in firmness, weight loss, surface color, and attractiveness are shown in Fig. 13 b-e. Fig. 13 b-d reveal that both the uncoated and R-CDs and PVA coated bananas started to exhibit browning and signs of rotting by the 8th day of storage, with these attributes worsening as time progressed. A similar observation is made with the R-CDs@PVA-coated bananas after 10 days of storage, as shown in Fig. 13 e. Potential microbial contamination is indicated by varying degrees of mixed bacterial infections found at the top or bottom of the bananas. Bananas wrapped in R-CDs@PVA, on the other hand, only developed black patches on their surface after ten days and had a pleasing appearance under the same storage conditions. This demonstrates the beneficial impact of the visible light-driven antibacterial functional films R-CDs@PVA on extending banana preservation. Furthermore, during storage, the unpackaged group (control) of bananas has a notable reduction in weight, which is mainly attributed to moisture evaporation and banana respiration. Because the cell wall components were preserved and the softening enzyme activity was regulated by the NCs, the bananas encased in the R-CDs@PVA softened the slowest. These findings indicate that the developed R-CDs@PVA visible-light-driven antibacterial NCs effectively shield bananas from microbial infections, thereby extending their shelf life.

ce:section.ce:section-title: R-CDs enriches the oxidative stress parameters in RBC model (In-vitro) via antioxidant potential
ce:section.ce:para: At RT, DPPH continues to exist as a stable free radical that can take an electron or a hydrogen radical [108]. Antioxidants tested in the DPPH assay prompt the conversion of the DPPH radical into its non-radical form, DPPH-H. As a result, absorption is decreased and the DPPH solutions purple color turns yellow. The ability of a substance to scavenge radicals is indicated by the amount of absorbance reduction at 517 nm. Di-Phenyl-2-picryl-Hdrazyl (DPPH) free radicals are efficiently scavenged by the R-CDs in a dose-dependent manner. It is discovered that the antioxidant potential is 75 % when compared to the positive control vitamin C, ascorbic acid. An illustration of the antioxidant activity of the R-CDs is provided in Fig. 14 a, where the mean IC50 value is 80.45 µg. Additionally, the antioxidant role is assessed by evaluating OS parameters induced by NaNO2 using the RBC model (in vitro) [109]. Indicators such as lipid peroxidation, protein carbonyl content, and total thiol content are utilized. Furthermore, antioxidant enzymes like SOD and CAT are employed to analyze OS. Lipid peroxidation is produced when polyunsaturated fatty acids are subjected to high concentrations of free radicals, leading to the production of compounds such as malanoldehyde (MDA), 4-hydroxy-2-nonenal, and acroline. We measured the quantity of MDA liberation in the presence of a TBA mixture as part of our investigations to estimate lipid peroxidation. It is interesting to note that R-CDs decreased MDA liberation in a dose-dependent way. Fig. 14 b indicate the R-CDs gradually decrease in MDA content as increase in the dose.

To find the elevated levels of protein carbonyl and TT content respectively, protein thiolation and carbonylation are also carried out. Compared with the ascorbic acid positive control, the R-CDs reduced the levels of PCC and TT in a dose-dependent manner ( Fig. 14 c & d). Additionally, an RBC model of OS induced by NaNO2 is used to measure the level of antioxidant enzymes [110,111]. At that time, the activity of antioxidant enzymes is lower due to stress overdose; as a result, the SOD and CAT activity is improved and the level of these enzymes is normalized by using R-CDs. SOD and CAT activity against OS caused by sodium nitrate is seen in ( Fig. 14 e & f).

In rat liver, kidney, pancreas, and heart homogenates, the levels of MDA, PCC and TT are notably raised after diclofenac injection (50 mg kg−1) compared to the positive control group treated with silymarin. Conversely, in homogenates from rats injected with R-CDs (150 mg kg−1), the stages of PCC, MDA, and TT are apparently lower than in the control groups. Nevertheless, levels of MDA, PCC and TT in rat homogenates treated solely with R-CDs remained within normal ranges compared to the control groups ( Fig. 15 a, b, & c). On the other hand, homogenates of rats given DFC show a substantial drop in natural antioxidant enzymes (CAT and SOD) [112–114]. When DFC is prepared for incubation with R-CDs (150 mg kg−1), anti-oxidant activity of enzymes is restored in the kidney, liver, heart and pancreas homogenates. SOD and CAT activities are unaffected in the R-CDs alone-treated groups as compared to the control groups indicated in Fig. 16 a & b).

ce:section.ce:section-title: R-CDs restored the morphological changes of liver, kidney, pancreas and heart affected by Diclofenac induced tissue damages
ce:section.ce:para: Diclofenac, categorized as a non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drug (NSAID), is commonly prescribed for alleviating inflammation and pain. The long term and continuous usage of drug (high dose) may cause high medication risk that cause severe damages to tissues. Long term usage of high dose diclofenac may lead to stomach cancer and chances of heart and kidney failures. The side effects of the drugs are the burning problems in the field of medication, the most concern of the researchers is to minimize the side effect caused by the drugs instead to use natural availing sources that enhance the body metabolism, immunity and reduce the level of pathological effect without any marked side effect. The R-CDs is examined on tissue morphology affected by diclofenac drug in rat model. Restoration of tissue damaging properties is examined on liver, kidney, pancreas and heart. From observation diclofenac treated R-CDs is restored the morphology of liver as compared to normal group, silymarin treated along with diclofenac animal groups does not show significant tissue damage whereas the animal groups treated with diclofenac alone is shown considerable tissue damage. The Fig. 17 a1–7 represent effect of diclofenac induced tissue damage and restoration of tissue damage by different concentration of R-CDs sample.

Histopathology of kidney is examined for R-CDs pre-treated diclofenac animal model. From the figure it is showed that, the kidney morphology of R-CDs pre-treated diclofenac animal groups is restored to normal infrastructure and silymarin pre-treated with diclofenac is observed there is no significant damage to the tissue but diclofenac alone treated animal group is shown marked tubular degeneration of kidney. The Fig. 17 b1–7 indicate R-CDs restored the morphological structure of kidney tissue damage induced by diclofenac in dose dependent manner. Histopathology of pancreas is examined by R-CDs pre-treated diclofenac, the morphological result suggested the restoration of damaged tissue architecture caused by diclofenac drug as compared with normal group. The silymarin pre-incubated diclofenac animal group is showed normal tissue architecture whereas the diclofenac drug treated animal group showed significant tissue damage. The Fig. 17 c1–7 indicate the effect of R-CDs on restoration of pancreases tissue damage caused by diclofenac. The tissue morphology of heart is studied by inducing tissues damage by using drug diclofenac. Interestingly the R-CDs pre-treated diclofenac animal groups showed considerable restoration of tissue damage. The silymarin pre-treated diclofenac group showed almost similar tissue architecture to the control group. The Fig. 17 d1–7 indicate the effect of R-CDs on diclofenac induced tissue damage rat model. Most importantly screening the level of toxicity of the samples play a vital role therapeutic area. Thus, the toxicity assessment of R-CDs is examined on HRBCs model. The erythrocytes are the essential components of the blood in the human physiological system and its role in distributing oxygen and nutrients to different body section. For this instance, the HRBCs were pre-incubated with R-CDs in buffer does not lyse the RBCs, it is shown that R-CDs does not toxic to HRBCs. The HRBCs were lysed by the positive control water, but they were maintained by the R-CDs (
Fig. 18 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: LED utilization in the growing of plants
ce:section.ce:para: LED efficacy could be significantly increased by excellent red FL of R-CDs. Upon these results, R-CDs are employed in the fabrication R-LEDs and W-LEDs. In the meantime, R-CDs are employed in PVA composites as a polymer matrix, which effectively inhibits the quenching effect of R-CDs due to aggregation [115]. The blue LED chip is subsequently coated with the nanocomposite, which is cured for 15 min at 80 °C to produce LEDs. The EL spectrum produced by R-LED is shown in Fig. 19 a. It shows that the highest luminescence wavelength is at 608 nm, and at an operational current of 20 mA, the luminous efficiency is 18.3 lm w−1. In contrast, the W-LEDs EL spectrum, as seen in Fig. 19 b, emits a warm, exceptional white light that covers the whole visible light spectrum. Additionally, the W-LED exhibits a Color Rendering Index (CRI) of 82, a Correlated Colour Temperature (CCT) of 4782 K, and a luminous efficiency of 37.6 lm w−1, demonstrating excellent performance in producing high-quality white light. Fig. 19 c displays the Commission International d’Eclairage (CIE) coordinates for the R-LED and W-LED, positioned at (0.525, 0.425) and (0.301, 0.319), respectively. To assess the effectiveness of the R-LED and W-LED, they were applied in indoor plant cultivation to demonstrate their ability to enhance plant growth. Chickpeas are the sample used in this investigation. To begin, chosen chickpea seeds are placed in plastic containers filled with soil. Next, three groups of plants are created (1, 2, and 3). Containers 1, 2, and 3 will be filled with proportions of manure and water across the course of the investigation. Sunlight is provided to all groups in order to facilitate photosynthesis through the day. At night, group 1 utilizes a plant light supplementary system to simulate sunlight, while groups 2 and 3 use W-LED and R-LED lights, respectively. The growth of the plants in all three groups is recorded daily from day 1 to day 10. As depicted in Fig. 19 d, both the white and red LEDs significantly enhance the overall photosynthesis of chickpeas. Chickpeas grown under R-LED lighting exhibit higher growth efficiency and stronger branches compared to those grown under sunlight and W-LED lighting. In order to examine the impacts of the various lighting conditions, we also evaluate the plant height, fresh weight, and leaf breadth of chickpeas. Group 3 plants have much higher heights, weights, and leaf widths than plants in groups 1 and 2, with the 2nd group being somewhat bigger over group 1, as seen in Fig. 19 e-g. The results of this study imply that cotyledon growth and photosynthesis can be greatly improved by both W-LED and R-LED.

In summary, we have presented a simple, environmentally friendly and cost-effective method for synthesizing R-CDs through a single-step ST treatment of Birch leaves. The synthesized R-CDs displayed the following characteristics: excellent solubility in ethanol, peak FL emission at 625 nm with a high QY of 16.5 %, and outstanding photo-stability. R-CDs mediate the synthesis of R-CDs@PVA NCs, which exhibit a significantly enhanced water-induced shape memory effect in comparison to pure PVA, by fostering the development of hydrogen bond-based physical crosslinking networks inside the PVA matrix. Moreover, the shape recovery ratio of NCs demonstrated a growing pattern as the pH of the water increased, indicating that the R-CDs-containing NC flexible sensibility can be attained when water is used as the stimulus. More importantly, incorporating long-wavelength emission labelling into PVA-based SMP proved advantageous for monitoring shape recovery in dark environments. This opens up promising opportunities for their utilization in various biological applications, including smart biosensors, stimuli-responsive drug-release systems, and innovative medical devices. Certainly, our research efforts have expanded to include the development of a fluorescent ink suitable for printing and writing, providing encryption and decryption functionalities to enhance security measures. The UV shielding effectiveness is enhanced by 1.5 times through adjusting the thickness to 250 μm, rather than increasing the concentration of UV shielding materials. A polymer film containing 0.08 wt% of R-CDs with a thickness of 250 μm is able to block ∼ 97.34, 96.71 and 97.69 % under all UV-A, UV-B and UV-C respectively. Hence, there is an expectation that polymer films dispersed with R-CDs@PVA (0.08 wt%) NCs could be explored as potential options for UV light filters to protect UV light-sensitive materials. The coating of R-CDs (0.08 wt%) improved shelf life by reducing fungal growth, spoilage, and moisture loss at RT. R-CDs have the ability to scavenge DPPH free radicals and superior antioxidant features, they protect RBCs from OS caused by NaNO2 and diclofenac. In addition, R-CDs exhibit anticoagulant and antiplatelet properties without having any negative side effects. Thus, R-CDs could be used to regulate and treat metabolic and inflammatory illnesses caused by OS. In the end, the R-CDs are used to create W-LEDs and R-LEDs. These LEDs show promise for solid-state LED applications as they function as excellent devices and successfully encourage plant development.

D Veera Vanitha: Investigation. H Nagabhushana: Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Radha krushna: Writing – original draft, Data curation, Conceptualization. Fr. Augustine George: Investigation. M. V. Manjula: Formal analysis. V. Shivakumar: Formal analysis. S. Devaraja: Formal analysis. S C Sharma: Supervision. K S Govardhan Rathla: Visualization. A. R. Srinivasan: Conceptualization. samir Sahu: Data curation. K Ponnazhagan: Formal analysis.
